speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: So on February the 1st, 1945, Jan and myself and a friend of my father, we three started out from Budapest on foot towards Miskolc, my home city. We went-- we walked about 60 kilometers. It took us, I think, three days or four days. We slept always in, in peasant houses. We had some bread with us. And I remember we had also some threads. These were a great article then. And we paid with this-- we didn't have money. And it was also very dangerous, because the Soviets had camps on different places. And whenever they saw people, they took them. So we had to go-- we had to make a great detour-- not to go on the shortest way-- a great detour, in snow up to knee-high.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so we went to a city, which is 60 kilometers from Budapest. And there, we managed to get in, in a, in a train, in a-- I forgot again-- what is it called? For merchandise? So anyway, anyway. And we managed to get to Miskolc the next morning. My cousin was already home. He was liberated earlier. And a short while after us, my father came by train. Already it was possible by train. He brought with him his best friend, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a relative of his best friend, another relative, another relative. My cousin had there his friends. So again, in our apartment, I don't know how many people lived, at least 10. Everybody came from some labor camp, or hiding, or camp or so. And was the apartment vacant? Was there nobody living in it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was a family. There was a family who moved in, after my family was taken away. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They stayed there in one room. And somehow, we slept. Again, just mattresses. And everybody was hungry. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I started to cook in the morning and cooked all day for everybody. Because everybody was eating and eating and eating, constantly. We were not that hungry, but the other people. At the same time, I went immediately to my previous school, the gymnasium, to the director. And I said that I want to finish my graduation, as soon as possible. So he arranged that. And I could graduate from high school July the 7th, 1945. During that time, Jan was partly in Miskolc. Then he went to Slovakia, to Preszow, then he came back again. And three days after the graduation, on July the 10th, '45, we got married. And a few days after that, we left for Slovakia, where he had to graduate from high school. After that, in end of September, I think it was, we went to Prague. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we both enrolled at the university. Now, I didn't speak one word Czech. So it was actually a chutzpah to enroll at the university. But Jan took me to the dean. And he said that this is my wife, we just got married. She's from Hungary. She doesn't speak Czech, but she would like to study.

speaker: interviewer
text: The dean was a very, very nice and very liberal man. And he said, is she Hungarian? How do I know that she was not a fascist? So we said, I couldn't be a fascist. I am Jewish, and I was persecuted, and so on and so forth.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, write down that you have Jewish nationality. Don't write Hungarian. And I can take you. But within one year, you have to do the-- several exams, some Czech literature, Czech history, and Czech geography-- what is taught in Czech high schools. So I did that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you learn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very, very difficult. Czech is a very difficult language. All the Slavic languages are difficult, and especially for a Hungarian. And I managed, I managed. After the year was off, I managed these three exams. And then I managed. It was very difficult, because Jan refused to talk Hungarian with me. He said, you must learn Czech. And he talked to me in Czech. And when friends came over who spoke both Hungarian and Czech, he forced everybody to speak Czech. They had a good time. I didn't know what is going on. He sent me everywhere. There was a vegetarian restaurant. I used to go there for lunch, and I didn't understand the menu. So I always put my finger on the first one. And once, it was carrots. And one, it was another name, but the same thing. And I always had the same thing, because I, I didn't understand anything. So he sent me everywhere. And it was a very strict. I also started to go to the theater. At first, I-- we didn't have much money as students. But I went up, up, up to the highest standing place. At first, I didn't understand anything. And then, it started to come. I understood what approximately what is going on. And slowly, slowly, I managed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after five years, I graduated, and I got my degree, which would be an MA here. This was a doctorate degree, but it would be an MA. At that time, we were already in Vienna.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think Jan talked about that. So he was in diplomatic service. I went there too, and I commuted. By the time I got my graduation diploma, I was already seven months pregnant. And in January 23, 1951, our daughter was born. But unfortunately, it was a very, very [SIGHS] difficult and unfortunate birth. And she was brain damaged by birth. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When Jan was recalled back to Prague eight months later, he went at first to Prague. And after a few days, when he knew that he's fired from the ministry, he sent me a telegram. Pack everything and come back to Prague.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was there with this eight-months-old very sick child. I had to pack the whole apartment and everything. And before we went to Vienna, we were students. So we had just a rented room. We didn't have anything. So we arrived to Prague. And we didn't have anywhere to go. We had to go to a hotel, a cheap hotel. There was one not very big room and a bathroom. There we managed to put up the crib.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jan already worked in the factory, every third week, night shifts. So he tried to sleep during the day, which was impossible. I couldn't go to work, because the child was so ill. So I took freelance translations from the Czechoslovak Press Agency. And I typed on my typewriter, on a tiny little table. On the radiator, there were the, the diapers and a tiny little electric cooker. I cooked carrots for the baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was how we lived half a year. After half a year, all our saved money went for the hotel. Because the hotel was terribly expensive. And you couldn't find a place to rent?

speaker: interviewee
text: We couldn't find a place. I took the child, who was then one year old. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had a paper from the factory where Jan worked, what is this-- what is his hourly wage. And I had a paper from the hotel, how much cost the room for one week. It was a great discrepancy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I went to this-- what is it called-- apartment office, apartment bureau? Where apartments were issued to people. And I went there, and I showed them these two papers. And I said, we don't have any money. I had 1,000 crowns, which was in-- before the money reform, so it was not that much money.

speaker: interviewee
text: I showed them, this is my last money for the last week rent in the hotel. The first week we are not paying, we will be on the street with this child. I said, I am not moving from here until you are giving us a livable apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then they remembered then they have one apartment, which is empty for three years. It was a divided apartment. We had the entrance hall and a toilet we had together with another family. They got two rooms, and we got two rooms, and the kitchen and the bathroom. I must drink. Please do. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewee
text: During the time that we lived in the hotel-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as I said, we had very, very little money, it was hardly enough for food-- I got ill. I had to go for an operation during this time. It was an extremely-- for me, maybe this, this half a year was the worst in my whole life. Finally, we got this apartment. We managed to put it somehow in over there. And we moved in. Jan still worked in the factory for seven years. In the meantime, he prepared for his doctorate degree. Our daughter died in August 22, 1953. She was two and a half years old. Two days later, Jan got his diploma. But he was still in the factory. I started to look for a job. And I started to work in January the 1st, '54, in the Academy of Sciences, in the Institute of Languages and Literatures. At that time, it was called Slavic Institute. But later on it became Languages and Literatures. And I was there as a Hungarian expert and for the-- for the Czech and Hungarian cultural relations. I had my degree from comparative literature, of comparative literature. At first, I worked as one of the editors of the Czech Hungarian big two-volume dictionary. At that time, I started to prepare for my PhD. I did the exams. And I started to write my doctoral thesis. And in 1962, I got my PhD.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, so we should have introduced you as Dr. Adam.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I am a doctor. Before that, in, in 1955, August-- I mean April the 19th, our second daughter was born, luckily healthy. And everything went in order.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Julie. And we were very, very happy. She has a very easy and very good child and very clever and very bright. So it was fine. In 1958, finally, they let Jan go from the factory. He couldn't get the job what was promised for him, so almost half a year he was unemployed. We lived from my salary. At that time, I didn't have yet my PhD, so my salary was rather low. So this was also a rather difficult--

speaker: interviewer
text: Trying times.

speaker: interviewee
text: Trying times. And, in addition, we had a child already. It was not just the two of us. Finally, January the 1st, 1959, he started to work at the university. So and then everything went fine. This was in '59. In '62, I got my PhD. So I, I worked then. Already the dictionary was finished. Then I worked, as I said, in literary and cultural relations. I published a great deal. I edited a volume of the This was published in Prague and in Budapest. And I wrote many articles and studies. In 1968, as it is well-known, August the 21st, the Soviet invasion came. [SIGHS] We woke up that Soviet tanks are in our street. And I think Jan talked about his trip to Montreal. This was already prepared. And Julie's trip was also prepared. Jan has relatives in England. And they invited Julie, that she should go to school there for half a year, to learn English. But then when the Soviets came, so we saw that this is the end of it. It's impossible to go anywhere. But then we heard that no, it is just impossible to fly from Prague, because the Soviets immediately occupied the airport. But by train, it is possible to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the two of them left. Jan said, you stay at home. We couldn't decide. It was extremely difficult.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we just bought, not even a year ago, a beautiful condominium, completely newly furnished, everything. We both had very good jobs. It was very difficult to make a jump into nothing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So Jan said, at first, I have to see what are the possibilities outside. Stay at home. He left by train with Julie to Vienna. And the next day from Vienna, he called me up-- that pack what you can and come to Vienna. Go to this and this address. This was an old friend of my father. And I stayed there. So they then left from Vienna. They flew to London. Jan left Julie with the relatives, and he flew to Montreal. I had half a day, a Saturday morning, to prepare for such a trip. I had to arrange everything what is possible. I mobilized my friends. I sent one to buy me a suitcase, because they took the suitcases. And the other one went to the train station, to buy me a ticket to Vienna, and so on and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: So finally, I managed, and I left for Vienna. I stayed with this old gentleman there. I got some money from the joint. And after three weeks, Jan wrote me from Montreal, so go to the Canadian consulate and arrange your trip. So I did that. And after three weeks, I got it. And I went to Montreal. So we were there. How did you arrive here? As refugees or landed immigrants?

speaker: interviewee
text: First as refugees. And later on, we got our landed immigrant status. I arrived on October the 13th, 1968. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And very soon after that, Jan got this position as a visiting professor at McGill. And I got my landed immigrant status in December. And right after that, Julie came. She flew by herself-- she 13 then-- to Montreal. And so we were together. But at that time, we already knew that we are going, at the end of the school year, to Calgary. And in June '69, we came to Calgary and--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do in Calgary? Did you work here as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, when I arrived to Canada, I didn't speak one word of English. So the first thing I did, I went to the McGill University and, and enrolled in a, a course of English for foreigners. So I did that while we were there. But the bad thing was that in Montreal, we had relatives and friends, either from Hungary or from Czechoslovakia. And they talk constantly Hungarian or Czech to me. So I didn't have too many opportunities. But in Calgary, of course, it was different. Because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had to speak English. So and I learned. Jan, very soon, about a week after we arrived here, he left for Berkeley. And we stayed here with Julie. And Jan told me-- so arrange the apartment. So we did that. And then Julie went to school. At first, I didn't do-- I helped a great deal to Jan. Because I had to type everything what he wrote. He worked on a research project at the same time and do all his lectures. So this was enough. And actually, this was the first time that I have-- I had time for Julie, that I didn't have to tell her, please leave me, I have to write this. I have to read this. Please just read something or do something-- leave me alone. Now, when at 4 o'clock, she came home from school, I was sitting there waiting for her. And, you know, as if you open a dam, just poured out from her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Special times.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was there for her. So I think this was a great plus. I think it was in '78 or '79-- I don't remember exactly-- I started to teach Hungarian, the language course of the Calgary school board. This went on for several years. And in 1983, I started to teach at the university, literature and culture of East European countries-- Czech, Polish, Russian, and Hungarian. And I did that till '88. Then I retired myself. And ever since, I am at home. And we are quite content and happy in Calgary. I think this is the first time in our lives that we can live not in a police state. And nobody's harassing us. And so we survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's great. Thank you very much. Do you have a message for your daughter, for future generations? A message because of your experience during the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just would like to say, first of all, for the few of us who survived and who are still living, never forget. Never forget what happened. And for the younger generation, do everything in your power that it should never happen again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my parents. This is their wedding picture in August the 30th, 1924.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were they married?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Miskolc, Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you get the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: All our family pictures remained in the house where we lived. And so after the war, we had it. And then we had it in Prague. And after we left Prague, some friends brought it out from our apartment and sent it via Holland to Calgary.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] Your father survived the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he managed to survive the war. And he-- after the war, he lived in Budapest. He then married again in 1950, I think. And he died in 80-- in 1986 in heart disease.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Budapest, in a hospital. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. OK. Who's in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother and myself. The picture was taken sometimes in the early '30s. I can be here, maybe seven or eight years old. I don't remember exactly. It was taken in Miskolc in Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, these are my parents. Sometimes in the '30s, I don't know exactly. They were on a holiday at the Lake Balaton in Hungary. It was taken there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling, who's in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our wedding picture, Jan and myself. July the 10th, 1945, in Miskolc, Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: You can sit here. It's hard to sit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the picture is our daughter, Julie. This was taken last year, on her 40th birthday, April the 19th, 1995, in Toronto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does she live in Toronto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she lives in Toronto.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a new camera. Looks different.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. This is our loaner for the day, because ours is giving us trouble. Camera's rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's that in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my maternal grandmother. Her name was Gisella Weinberger. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know exactly how old was she on this picture. But she was 73 when she was deported, in 1944. This is my maternal grandfather. His name was Jeno Weinberger. He died in Miskolc in 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's that in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my paternal grandparents, Katalina and Jakob Klein. The grandmother died in Miskolc in 1940. And my grandfather was deported in 1944 and perished in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my parents and myself, as a baby. So it must have been taken in 1925, in Miskolc, Hungary.
